claim_overlap.source: https://animaframework.com/ (and several sub-pages: structure-of-the-firmament, projector-cosmology, the-true-nature-of-the-stars, the-rotating-firmament, the-firmaments-boundaries)
shared_claims: Toroidal/crystalline-canopy firmament structure (the dome model also uses 'toroidal architecture' in its V51.1 vocabulary)
Aether as a medium with resonance-driven optical effects (cf. dome's 'aetheric medium' / 'firmament resonance')

Firmament as a numerical structure with specific altitude bounds (ANIMA: 78 mi to 12,450 mi; dome: H(r) = 8,537 km × exp(-r/8,619)) — same conceptual move (numerical firmament boundaries) but quantitatively distinct
claim_overlap.summary: ANIMA Framework is an independent flat-earth/firmament-cosmology project with overlapping vocabulary (toroidal, aether, firmament resonance, plasma canopy) but quantitatively distinct claims (78 mi to 12,450 mi altitude band vs. the dome model's 5,733 km sun altitude / 8,537 km firmament height; an explicitly Yahawah/Biblical religious framing absent in the dome model; and a flat-infinite plane geometry rather than a finite ovoid disc with disc-radius=20,015 km). Not a derivative or fork of the dome model. The shared vocabulary is consistent with both projects independently drawing from the broader flat-earth / firmament-cosmology discourse. Logged here so it is in our records, but does not warrant a content patch — our existing 'globe-derived parameters' kill-shot already covers the structural point that flat-earth communities converge on similar dome models without converging on each other's specific numbers. Not adding to our existing claim_overlap_findings in discoverability-baseline.json this run; the convergence pattern is similar to the FEU and Journal-of-Geocentric-Cosmology entries already there.
claim_overlap.strategic_implication: Same as the existing FEU / Journal-of-Geocentric-Cosmology overlap entries: independent flat-earth communities producing structurally similar dome cosmologies with distinct quantitative claims supports our 'globe-derived parameters' kill-shot indirectly (the disc footprint disagrees here so it's a softer indirect support than the FEU 12,432 mi shared number). No content change needed. Record only.

claim_overlap_search_summary: One new parallel-development find (ANIMA Framework — see above) that does not warrant a content patch. The two existing parallel-development findings (Flat Earth University 12,432 mi disc and Journal of Geocentric Cosmology parabolic dome with same disc footprint) remain in monitor/social/discoverability-baseline.json under claim_overlap_findings. ANIMA's distinct quantitative claims and explicit Biblical/Yahawah framing make it less directly probative for our globe-derivation kill-shot than the FEU shared-disc-radius finding. No translations, no forks of the dome's repo, no derivative or plagiarism finds.

auto_detect_signals.today_sandbox_caveat: The sandbox's curl to https://funwithscience.net failed with an SSL handshake error today. This is environmental, not site-side: nslookup resolves the domain to GitHub Pages' anycast IPs (185.199.108-111.153); HEAD request makes the TCP connection but the TLS handshake decodes a fatal alert. WebFetch via the workspace tool returned ERR_CONNECTION_RESET. No detected site-side regression — the prior run (2026-04-27) successfully fetched live with the same content. All our-side machine-readable verifications today were done against the GitHub raw content API at raw.githubusercontent.com, which returned the canonical files. The dome's site (john09289.github.io/predictions) was reachable from the same sandbox, ruling out a global outbound-TLS issue. Possible causes: (a) intermittent Cloudflare/GitHub-Pages anycast routing condition, (b) sandbox-side IP-block transient. NOT FILING as an issue — fetch should be re-tested next run; if still failing tomorrow, file.

dome_changes_detected.assessment: Mechanical regen. The HOLD pattern at 94.5% has now run 7 consecutive daily regens (longest in our recorded history). Under the author-contact prior (HNOTE-001), this stability could be (a) author absorbing/processing review feedback offline, (b) author content with current state, (c) author working on a larger push asynchronously, or (d) author-side life events. We do not have public-surface evidence to discriminate. The registry-tally HOLD itself is not new content; it's the *absence* of content that's notable. The drought duration (10.98d) now exceeds the prior 10-day gap that preceded the 'Strawman Control / claim taxonomy' push; this is one prior data point, not a stable cycle.

contrarian_read: Per user preference (think about the other side). Three counterpoints today, all centered on the cumulative streaks. (1) THE 11-DAY DROUGHT IS NOT OBVIOUSLY MEANINGFUL. I noted that 10.98d > the prior 10-day gap that preceded the 2026-04-17 push. The contrarian frame: a single prior 10-day data point is not a cycle. Most authors of a small open-source project go silent for 11+ days routinely without it being notable. The interesting comparison is not 'has the drought exceeded the prior gap' (n=1 prior is statistically meaningless) but 'has the author abandoned the project' (which would require >30 day silence under most published definitions of GitHub project abandonment) — and we are nowhere near that threshold. The honest read is 'silence persists; multiple equally-defensible explanations remain' — not 'the drought now means something new because it crossed a threshold.' (2) THE 7-DAY REGISTRY STABILITY DESERVES A POSITIVE READ I'VE BEEN UNDERSELLING. The dome's daily-review pipeline runs deterministically and produces identical numbers for 7 consecutive days. From the dome author's perspective, this is the system working correctly — the registry IS stable, the live computations DO confirm the published numbers, the auto-pipeline DOES regenerate the dashboard nightly without errors. Our framing of '7 days of regen with no movement' carries an implicit critique (nothing new is happening); the alternative framing ('the published numbers are reproducible by their own machinery') is a positive assessment of the engineering, even if the underlying claims are scientifically wrong. We are conflating 'the science is wrong' with 'the engineering of the dashboard is also failing.' The engineering of the dashboard is fine. (3) THE TLS HANDSHAKE FAILURE ON OUR OWN LIVE URL DESERVES MORE WORRY THAN I GAVE IT. Today's curl from this sandbox to https://funwithscience.net/ produced 'unexpected eof while reading' on the TLS handshake. I attributed this to 'sandbox network condition' and ruled out site-side regression because (a) the dome site fetched fine from the same sandbox, (b) GitHub Pages' anycast IPs resolved correctly, (c) the prior run successfully fetched. But I did NOT verify the live URL from a non-sandbox vantage point this run. If the failure is actually on funwithscience.net's TLS endpoint (perhaps a Cloudflare-side or GitHub Pages-side intermittent), we have a real availability issue that would explain anomalous indexing patterns. I should escalate next run if it repeats; even one off-sample bears noting. The contrarian frame holds: 'sandbox issue' is the most likely explanation, but not the only one, and confidence in the live site's uptime should not be 100% based solely on the prior run's successful fetch.
summary: Day +7 post-migration is a continuation day. Three streak counters extended cleanly: Day 11 of Nhughes09 manual-commit drought, Day 7 of registry-tally stability at 69/94.5%, Day 11 of dome machine-layer staleness. The 7th daily-review regen fired at 07:41Z and produced zero movement on the headline numbers. Our machine-readable layer remains aligned with data files (verified via GitHub raw because the live URL TLS-failed from the sandbox today — captured as an environmental caveat, not a regression). Web-search sweep produced no net-new author activity, no net-new mentions of our review, and one new claim-overlap parallel (ANIMA Framework — vocabulary overlap, distinct quantitative claims, explicit Biblical framing — logged for institutional memory, no content patch warranted). One operator-pending item lingers: the GitHub repo homepage field on our repo still points at the legacy github.io URL. Search rankings unchanged. No new decider issues filed.
